html:
III

Now they had left the high-road and were making southeast through the winding lanes. Their shoulders were turned to the sea, though in that lost world of the mist only the native could tell where the bay was supposed to lie. It was one of the dead hours, too, when even the salt goes out of the marsh-air, and no pulse in it warns you subconsciously of the miracle coming. Between the high-mounted hedges it was still and close, and beyond them the land rose until its dank green surface stood soft against the sky. All the way Simon looked at the land with a critical eye, the eye of the lover which loves and asks at the same time. He looked at the ploughland and knew the rotation through which it had run and would have to run again; at rich grassland which seemed never to have known the steel, and fields which, at rest for a hundred years, still spoke to some long-rusted share. He loved it, but he thought of it first and foremost as good material for the good workman engaged on the only job in the world. It was always the land that he coveted when he came to Blindbeck, never the house. Eliza had made of the house a temple to the god of Blessed Self-Satisfaction, but even Eliza could not spoil the honest, workable land.

The farm kept showing itself to them as they drove, a quadrangle of long, well-kept buildings backed by trees. When the sun shone, the white faces of house and shippon looked silver through the peeping-holes of the hedge, but today they were wan and ghostly in the deadening mist. The turned beeches and chestnuts were merely rusty, instead of glowing, and seemed to droop as if with the weight of moisture on their boughs. The Scotch firs on a mound alone, stark, straight, aloof, had more than ever that air of wild freedom which they carry into the tamest country; and the pearly shadow misting their green alike in wet weather or in dry, was today the real mist, of which always they wear the other in remembrance.

The farm had its back well into the grassy hill, and the blind river which gave it its name wound its way down to it in a hidden channel and went away from it in a hidden dip in a field below. There was water laid on at Blindbeck, as Sarah knew, with a copper cylinder in a special linen-room, and a hot towel-rail and a porcelain bath. SimonвАЩs particular envy was the electric light, that marvel of marvels on a northern farm. He never got over the wonder of putting his hand to the switch, and seeing the light flash out on the second to his call. Once he had sneaked out of the house on a winterвАЩs night, and in the great shippon had turned the lights on full. Eliza, of course, had been nasty about it when she heard, but Will had understood him and had only laughed. Later, swinging a lantern in his own dark shippon, Simon had thought of those switches with envious longing. He did not know that they had taken the warm glamour out of the place, and slain in a blow the long tradition of its beauty. The lantern went with him like a descended star as he moved about, and out of the cattleвАЩs breath wove for itself gold-dusted halos. There had been something precious about it all before, some sense of mystery and long-garnered peace, but tonight he could only remember Blindbeck and its modern toy. For the time being he ceased to feel the pull of the sweetest chain in the world, which runs straight back through all the ages to the Child in the Bethlehem Stall.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ There was a billiard-table at Blindbeck, too, with more switches to tempt Simon, and a well-laid tennis-lawn in the neat garden by the stream. On the far side of the farm was a great highway running north and south, as well as a mainline station over the drop of the hill. It seemed as if everything was made easy for those who lived at Blindbeck, from the washing of pots and the moving of stock to the amusement and education of the bairns.

Folk who came to Blindbeck for the first time believed that at last they had found the farm of all their dreams. They called it an Earthly Paradise, a model miniature village, a moral object-lesson, a True Home. They came to it between well-cropped fields, marked by trim hedges and neat stone walls, and through uniformly painted gates secure in hinge and hasp into a tidy yard. They looked with pleasure at the shining knocker on the green house-door and the fruit tree lustily climbing the warm south wall. They looked with delight at the healthy, handsome family, the well-placed buildings and the show of pedigree stock. They looked at Will as he went shyly by, and said that his wife was undoubtedly the better horse. They looked at Eliza and said that she was the Housewife of Romance. When they went away they told others of this Paradise which was Blindbeck, and the others came in their turn and looked and said the same. But to Simon and Sarah it was plain Purgatory and nothing else, and with each gate that they loosed they unloosed a devil as well.

There was a party at Blindbeck this afternoon, as long custom might have led them to expect. It was part of ElizaвАЩs Method to gather a party together when the poor relations were due. There was always a noisy crowd, it seemed to the Simons, when they were tired, or when they had any particular business to transact. On the day after the lads had flown there had been an unusually large crowd, with faces that looked like masks to the parentsвАЩ tired eyes.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Will was fond of young folk, and made no objection to the stream of вАЬcompanyвАЭ passing beneath his roof. His shy, quiet eyes watched the young tide of life surging ahead, with Eliza floundering like a porpoise in its midst. He was content only to watch, but he was not stranded, like the thirsty Simons; the waves still lapped about his feet. He could see youth and the pride of youth without the sense of desolation which embittered his brother and took his brotherвАЩs wife by the throat. Simon was always surly when he came to Blindbeck, while Sarah was like a bomb in the hand which any unconscious soul might throw. Will did not know that for them every lad that they looked at should have been Geordie, and each lass a lass of their own with GeordieвАЩs face. He was sorry and sympathetic, but he did not know those things. It was Eliza who knew, and used the knowledge for her private ends. You could always be sure that Eliza knew where your hidden things were kept.

Today, tired as they were with the hours in town, and already reacting from their great decision, a jovial party seemed more than they could stand. Signs of it reached them as they came to the last gate, making Sarah draw in her lips and Simon scowl. The sounds seemed intensified by the stillness of the day, crossing and jarring the mood of Nature as well as that of the approaching guests. Faces were pressed to panes as they rattled up, but nobody came out to give Sarah a hand down, or to offer to help Simon with the horse. They were too common a sight to arouse any interest or even courtesy in that house.

She climbed down gropingly, and he led the horse away, leaving her standing, waiting, in the empty yard. She stood with her back turned to the kitchen window, conscious, though she could not see them, of the eyes that were raking her shabby figure through the glass. The sounds of merriment burst out afresh, and she winced a little, though she did not move. They were laughing at her, she felt sure, but there was nothing new to that. They often laughed, she knew, since she had ceased to be able to stop them with a glance. She shivered, standing there, and her bones ached with the damp, but she was in no hurry to enter the warm, crowded room. It was better to shiver in the coldest spaces of earth than to be shut into Heaven itself with Eliza and her tongue.

The green house-door with its brass knocker was close at her left hand, but she did not attempt to open it and go in. That was a privilege only accorded to the rich and proud, not to a poor relation come to beg. Nevertheless, it was one of her hidden dreams that someday she would enter by that grand front-door. In the Great Dream Geordie came home with a fortune in his hands, so that all doors, even the Door of Blindbeck, instantly stood wide. They would drive up to it in a smart cart behind a fast young horse, with Geordie, a pattern of fashion, holding the reins. His mother would be beside him, of course, in crackling silk, with a velvet mantle and a bonnet of plumes and jet. Simon, the lesser glory, would have to sit behind, but even Simon would be a sight for Blindbeck eyes. When the Dream came true, the house could be as full of pryers as it chose, with crushed noses and faces green with envy set like bottle-ends in every pane. The farm-men would come to the doors and gape, and even the dogs would stop to sniff at so much that was new. Geordie would jump down, reins in hand, and bang the brass knocker until it shook the house, while Sarah, secure in the presence of her golden lad, would sit aloft and aloof like any other silken queen. Soon they would hear ElizaвАЩs step along the sacred, oil-clothed passage; and she, when she opened the door, would see their glory framed beyond. Sarah would throw her a graceful word, asking leave to step inside, and climb down with a rustle of silk on the arms of her husband and son. She would set her feet on the snowy steps and never as much as trouble to look for a mat. With a smile she would offer her hostess a kindly, kid-gloved hand. In the whole armour of the successful mother she would bear down upon her foe.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

It was one of those things that seem as if they might happen so easily, and never doвБ†вАФnever do. Simon returned presently, accompanied by Will, and they entered the house as usual through the old stone porch. No dog even looked aside at them as they crossed to the kitchen door. No portent of coming wonder shed a sudden sunlight on the day. The old trap was tipped on its shafts behind a sheltering wall. The old horse, himself mere waiting food for the nearest hounds, munched his way happily through his feed of Blindbeck corn.

Will talked shyly as he led the way, trying to brighten the melancholy pair.

вАЬYou must have a sup oвАЩ tea before we get to business,вАЭ he said to his brother, вАЬand Sarah can rest herself while we have our crack. WeвАЩre over soon wiвАЩ tea today, but I reckon you wonвАЩt mind that. YouвАЩll be tired likely, and itвАЩs none so warm. IвАЩll be bound SimonвАЩll have a thirst on him anyway!вАЭ he smiled to Sarah. вАЬHeвАЩs done a deal oвАЩ tattling, Simon has, today!вАЭ

He could not get any response from them, however; indeed, they scarcely seemed to hear. The fear of Eliza was upon them, that was always so strong until they were actually in her presence, the same fear that had sent them scuttling like scared rabbits out of the Witham inn. Sarah was struggling with the usual jealous ache as they entered the spacious, cleanly place, with the kindly smell of new-baked bread filling the whole house. She knew as well as the mistress where the kitchen things were kept, the special glories such as the bread-maker, the fruit-bottler, and the aluminium pans. The Blindbeck motto had always been that nothing beats the best. Half her own tools at home were either broken or gone, and there was only a blind woman to make shift with the rest as well as she could. Little need, indeed, for a great array, with the little they had to cook; and little heart in either cooking or eating since Geordie had gone away.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

Will opened the door of the main kitchen, and at once the warmth and jollity sweeping out of it smote the shrinking visitors like an actual blast. The party were already at table, as he had said, and met the latecomers with a single, focused stare. It was one of their chief bitternesses, indeed, that they always seemed to arrive late. Eliza was at the back of it, they felt almost sure, but they had never been able to discover how. No matter how they hurried the old horse, asked the hour of passersby, or had SimonвАЩs old watch put as right as it would allow, they never seemed to arrive at the right time. They could not be certain, of course, that she had watched for them from upstairs, and at the first sign of their coming had hustled the party into tea, but somehow or other they knew it in their bones. Things happened like that, they would have told you, when you were up against Mrs.¬†Will; things that never by any chance would have happened with anybody else.

The room was cloudy to Sarah as she went in, but jealousy had long ago printed its details on her mind. She knew what the vivid wallpaper was like, the modern furniture and the slow-combustion grate. Once it had been a beautiful old houseplace with a great fire-spot and a crane, an inglenook, a bacon-loft, and a chimney down which both sun and moon could slant a way. Eliza, however, had soon seen to it that these absurdities were changed, and Sarah, though she affected contempt, approved of the changes in her heart. It was true that she always returned to Sandholes with a great relief, but she did not know that its bare austerity soothed her finer taste. She only knew that her mind expanded and her nerves eased, and, though grief went with her over every flag and board, a cool hand reached to her forehead as she went in.

Simon included in one surly glance the faces round the loaded table, the bright flowers, the china with the gilded rim, and the new window-curtains which he would never even have seen in any house but this. вАЬPlush, by the look on вАЩem, and the price of a five pun note!вАЭ he thought resentfully, as he stood waiting to be given a place, and wondering which of the people present he disliked the most. There were the two Swainson lasses from the nearest farm, with their young duke of a brother, who was in a Witham bank. There was a Lancashire youth whom Will had taken as pupil, and Stephen Addison and his missis, who were both of them preaching-mad. He held forth at chapel and she at Institute meetings and the like, and folk said they kept each other awake at nights, practising which of them could do it best. There was Sam Battersby of Kitty Fold, who never knew where his own heaf ended and other peopleвАЩs began, and the familiar smug cousin, long since formally pledged to ElizaвАЩs eldest lass. There was a grandchild or two, and of course the Blindbeck brood, with the exception of a couple of married daughters and the obliterated Jim.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ It was small wonder, indeed, that, after all those years, nobody missed him in that upcoming crowd.

ElizaвАЩs hearty voice, that was never hearty at core, rose like a strong-winged, evil bird at the unwanted guests. The sight of them seemed to surprise her so much that she dropped a gold-rimmed cup.

вАЬSurely to goodness, Simon and Sarah, yonвАЩs never you! IвАЩd give you up an hour back or more, I had indeed. YouвАЩve been a terble while on tвАЩroad, surelyвБ†вАФa terble while after us? But thereвБ†вАФI always forget how fast yon grand little mare of ours gets over tвАЩground! YouвАЩd need to start sooner than most folk wiвАЩ your poor old crock.вАЭ

She broke off to throw a remonstrance at Will, who was bundling two of his daughters out of their seats to make room for their uncle and aunt.

вАЬNay, now, Will,вАЭ she called vexedly down the table. вАЬWhat dвАЩye think youвАЩre at? Leave tвАЩlasses alone, canвАЩt you? Let the poor things be! If itвАЩs a chair youвАЩre wanting, thereвАЩs one here by me asвАЩll suit Sarah just grand. Sarah canвАЩt abide a chair wiвАЩ a cane bottomвБ†вАФsays it rubs her gown. ItвАЩs right enough, too, IвАЩm sure, wiвАЩ velvet and the likeвБ†вАФ(I made a bonny mess oвАЩ yon grand gown I had when Annie Belle was wed)вБ†вАФbut I canвАЩt see as itвАЩll do any harm to a bit oвАЩ poorish serge. Anyway, Sarah can have the best plush to set on, if she sets here, and, as for Simon, youвАЩre forever sticking him where I canвАЩt so much as see the end of his nose! YouвАЩre never thinking IвАЩm still sweet on him, surely,вАЭ she added, laughing, вАЬor that happen heвАЩll be making sheepвАЩs eyes at me, as he used to do?вАЭ

She looked at the young folk, and chuckled and winked, and they nudged each other and laughed, too. But Sarah did not laugh as she waited behind the chairs, or Simon, red to the ears, and recalling the machinations of ElizaвАЩs youth. He pushed one of his nieces roughly out of his way and took her place, while Sarah went slowly to seat herself on the red plush chair that was warranted not to hurt her poor patched gown.

вАЬI hope thereвАЩs summat for you, IвАЩm sure!вАЭ Eliza went on, when the giggling and whispering had died down, and SimonвАЩs thin cheeks had lost their furious red. She cast an anxious glance down the well-filled table, but her tone was complacency itself. вАЬFolks as come late canвАЩt expect to find everything just so.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Ay, I give you up a long while back. Sally hereвАЩll tell you I give you up. вАШSally,вАЩ I says to her, вАШlikely yon old horseвАЩll be put to it to do the extra bit, and so theyвАЩve happen thought better onвАЩt, and gone straight home. YouвАЩre that used to good horses, Sally,вАЩ I says, вАШyou donвАЩt rightly know how poor folks has to shift. Not but what theyвАЩll get a deal better tea here than they will at home, Sally,вАЩ I says, вАШand though I says it as shouldnвАЩt, thatвАЩs the truth! Ay, theyвАЩll come to tea, IвАЩll be bound, Sally,вАЩ I says, but I changed my mind when I thought on the old horse.вАЭ

Sarah said nothing in reply to this, partly because her brain was swimming with the heat of the room, but chiefly because she never did say anything until Eliza was well ahead in the race for speech. This particular method helped her to reserve her strength, but at the same time it deepened the bitterness in her heart. It would have been better for both of them if they could have got the inevitable tussle over at the start; exhaustion on both sides might have brought at least a pretence at amity in its train. But it had always been SarahвАЩs instinct to hold herself back, and time had turned the instinct into a fixed need. For the moment, at least, her strength was certainly to sit still.

вАЬI doubt thereвАЩs no tea for you just this minute, Sarah,вАЭ Eliza said, affecting great concern as she lifted the teapot lid. вАЬSally, my lass, youвАЩd best see about mashing another pot. ThereвАЩll be a deal oвАЩ folk sending up for more in a brace oвАЩ shakes, and we canвАЩt have them saying theyвАЩre not as well-tret at Blindbeck as theyвАЩre used. Not as anybodyвАЩs ever said it yet as IвАЩve heard tell, though you never know what folksвАЩll do for spite. Most on вАЩem get through their three cups afore theyвАЩre done, and me like as not just barely through my first. Eh, but I used to be terble bothered, just at the start, keeping folks filled and their mugs as they rightly should! You bairns wasnвАЩt up then, of course, but weвАЩd farm-lads in the house, and wiвАЩ a rare twist to вАЩem anвАЩ all! YonвАЩs a thing youвАЩve never been bothered with, Sarah, wiвАЩ such a small spot and lile or nowt in the way oвАЩ work. YouвАЩd nobbut a couple oвАЩ hands at any time, had you, and not them when youвАЩd Geordie-anвАЩ-Jim? YouвАЩve a deal to be thankful for, IвАЩm sure, you have that! YouвАЩve always been able to set down comfortable to your meat, instead oвАЩ fretting yourself to skin and bone seeing as other folk had their wants.вАЭ

Here Mrs.¬†Addison offered to pass her cup, and then thought better of it, remembering the new brew. Eliza, however, urged it forward. Apparently she had discovered concealed virtue under the teapot lid.

вАЬNay, now, Mrs.¬†Addison, thereвАЩs a sup in the pot yet! YouвАЩve no call to look shy about itвБ†вАФI wasnвАЩt talking at you!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Pass Mrs.¬†Addison the cream, Mary Phyllis, and waken up and look sharp about it! Blindbeck teaвАЩs none the worse, I reckon, for a drop oвАЩ Blindbeck cream.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ She returned the cup, smiling benignly, and then pretended to have lost Sarah and suddenly found her again. вАЬLosh, Mrs.¬†Simon, youвАЩre that whyet IвАЩd clean forgot you were there! YouвАЩll not want to be waiting on Sally and the fresh brew. IвАЩll wet leaves again for you just to be going on with!вАЭ

So Sarah got the bottom of the pot after a little more talk, a hunt for a clean cup and an address on the value of the spoons. Half a cupвБ†вАФconsisting chiefly of tea-leavesвБ†вАФwas passed to Simon, but was intercepted on its way by Will. Simon did not notice the manoeuvre, being busy glowering at a nieceвАЩs shoulder turned sulkily on him from the left; but Eliza saw it from her end of the table and turned an angry red. She never forgot SimonвАЩs indifference to her as a girl, and would have made him pay for the insult if she could. She could not always reach him, however, because of the family tie which nothing seemed able to break. But Sarah, at least, it was always consoling to think, could be made to pay. There were times when all her reserve could not hide from a gleeful Eliza that she paid.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

So Simon got the new brew without even knowing that it was new, while Sarah drank the unpleasant concoction that was weak at the top and bitter as seawater at the bottom. Sally came in with another great brown pot, and sat down languidly at her auntвАЩs side. She and the smug cousin had been engaged for years, but there seemed little prospect of the wedding taking place. She had been a handsome girl, and was good to look at still, but there were handsomer Thornthwaites growing and grown up, as apparently the cousin was quick enough to perceive. Today he had found a seat for himself beside Mary Phyllis, who kept glancing across at her sister with defiant pride. Sally had a cheap town-look nowadays, the cousin thought, not knowing that she had assumed it long ago to please himself. Now that he was more mature, he preferred the purer country type of Mary Phyllis, as well as the fresher atmosphere of her youth. Sally talked to young Swainson, and pretended not to care, but she was too unhappy to bother about her aunt. The Simon Thornthwaites were boring at any time, like most permanently unlucky people, and today she was too worried even to try to be kind. So Sarah, after whom she was called, and who was her godmother to boot, got very little to eat and only the dregs of things to drink; and nobody at all rose up to deliver her from Eliza.

Mrs.¬†Addison had opened her mouth very impressively more than once, but it was only now that she got a chance to speak. In spite of their boasted fluency, both she and her husband had always to yield the palm to Mrs.¬†Will. Mrs.¬†Addison, however, always watched her chance, while Stephen was simply flabby, and did not try. She and Eliza in the same room were like firmly opposing currents, flowing strongly in the same stream.

вАЬMr.¬†AddisonвАЩs to preach at this mission theyвАЩre having, next week,вАЭ she announced proudly. вАЬThereвАЩs to be a Service for men only, and our StephenвАЩs to give вАЩem a talk. I wonвАЩt say but what heвАЩll do as well as a real minister, even though I do happen to be his wife. Likely youвАЩll think on about it, and send some of your lads along, Mrs.¬†Will?вАЭ

Eliza was quite unable to conceal her disgust at a distinction achieved by somebody not her own.

вАЬIвАЩll do my best, IвАЩm sure,вАЭ she assented casually and without looking at her, вАЬthough I doubt theyвАЩll want coaxing a bit wiвАЩ a broom-handle or a clout!вАЭ She disliked being called Mrs.¬†Will, and knew that Mrs.¬†Addison did it with fell intent. It was galling to be reminded that, in spite of his success, Will had still not managed to make himself into the elder son.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬI canвАЩt say theyвАЩre that set on either church or chapel unless itвАЩs to see a lass,вАЭ she went on, busy with the cups, вАЬand I doubt they donвАЩt reckon much oвАЩ sermons unless theyвАЩre good. TheyвАЩve been better eddicated than most folk, youвАЩll think on, so theyвАЩre hard to suit. вАЩTisnвАЩt likely they could do wiвАЩ secondhand preaching from some as happen never went to school at all.вАЭ

Mr.¬†Addison made a sudden attempt to speak, but choked instead, while Eliza looked as innocent as a large-sized lamb.

вАЬAy, IвАЩve heard a deal oвАЩ sermons as was just waste breath,вАЭ she went on kindly, вАЬand thatвАЩs the truth. All the same, IвАЩll likely look in at Mission myself, one oвАЩ these days, if I can get away. IвАЩm always glad to set still after a hard week, and to get a look at other folksвАЩ jackets and hats. Not that thereвАЩs much to crack on at chapel, that way.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ IвАЩm a deal fonder oвАЩ church. I was wed at St.¬†MichaelвАЩs, youвАЩll think onвБ†вАФay, and Sarah anвАЩ all. Eh, I could laugh even yet at yon march we stole on her, me anвАЩ Will!вАЭ

Sally moved impatiently at her auntвАЩs elbow, and muttered something under her breath. She was tired of the old story, and disapproved of it as well. Sarah had lifted her cup to her lips, but now she set it down.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

Mary Phyllis stopped giggling a moment, and leaned forward to speak.

вАЬI was telling Cousin Elliman about it only this morning,вАЭ she said noisily, вАЬand he says itвАЩs the funniest thing he ever heard! I thought everybody knew about it, but he says he didnвАЩt. He said it was real smart of you, Mother, and he wished he could have been there.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩll be bound Sarah didnвАЩt think it smart!вАЭ Eliza chuckled, but without glancing at her victimвАЩs face. She had a trick of discussing people when they were present, as Sarah knew. She could tell by the trend of ElizaвАЩs voice that she spoke without turning her head.

вАЬSmart? Nay! Sarah was real wild, you take my word! I spoke to her in tвАЩvestry when the show was through, and she give me a look as was more like a dogвАЩs bite. Eh, well, I reckon poor Sarah was jealous oвАЩ my gown, seeing her own was nowt to crack onвБ†вАФand nowt then! IвАЩd always settled to be real smart when I got wed, and my own lasses was just the same. None oвАЩ my folk can do wiвАЩ owt as isnвАЩt first-class and happen a bit over. YonвАЩs the photo we had took at Annie BelleвАЩs wedding,вАЭ she added, turning to point, вАЬand thereвАЩs another of Alice EvelynвАЩs in the parlour.вАЭ

The cousin and Mary Phyllis left their seats to giggle together over the stiff figures, and presently the girl turned to her sister with a malicious taunt.

вАЬI say, our Sally, youвАЩd best look out when you do get wed, or happen IвАЩll play a trick on you, same as mother did Aunt Sarah! YouвАЩll be rarely riled if I come marching up the aisle with a fine young man, taking all the shine out of you and Elliman!вАЭ

The cousin said something in a low tone which made her flush and laugh, and Sally guessed at it quickly enough, though it did not reach her ears. The tears came into her eyes, and on an impulse of fellow-feeling she turned towards her aunt. She was asking after May Fleming when her mother broke across her talk.

вАЬEh, now, Sarah, yon was never May, was it, along wiвАЩ you in Witham? IвАЩll be bound IвАЩd never have known her if she hadnвАЩt been with you, but thereвАЩs not that many youвАЩre seen about with nowadays at market. вАЩTisnвАЩt like me, as canвАЩt stir a step without somebody wanting a crack or hanging on to my gown. But MayвАЩs changed out of all knowledgeвБ†вАФI was fair bothered to see her look so old! IвАЩll swear our Annie Belle looks as young again, for all sheвАЩs been wed a dozen year at least. Ay, I thought May terble old, and terble unmannerly as well. IвАЩd be shammed to think as any lass oвАЩ mine had suchlike ways. You werenвАЩt over-pleasant spoken yourself, Sarah, if it comes to that. The folk in the caif were laughing a deal after youвАЩd gone out, and saying you must be wrong in the garrets to act so queer.вАЭ

Sarah had regained her spirit a little, in spite of her poor tea. She straightened herself on the plush chair and answered calmly.

вАЬThey can say what suits вАЩem and welcome, as long as they let me be. You know what put me about, Eliza, and nobody to thank for it but yourself. As for folks laughing and making game oвАЩ me and suchlike, it was you they was sniggering at plain enough when I come out.вАЭ

ElizaвАЩs colour rose, but she struggled to keep her virtuous air. She looked at Sarah with a sorrowful eye.

вАЬI wouldnвАЩt get telling lies about it, Sarah,вАЭ she observed kindly, вАЬI wouldnвАЩt indeed! Mrs.¬†AddisonвАЩs listening, think on, and sheвАЩll be rarely shocked at suchlike ways. Caif-folk were shocked more than a deal, anвАЩ me just having a friendly talk anвАЩ all!вАЭ

вАЬItвАЩs a queer sort oвАЩ friendliness as puts folk to open shame!вАЭ SarahвАЩs colour was flying a flag, too. вАЬItвАЩs nobbut a queer sort oвАЩ friend as goes shouting your private business at the end of a bell!вАЭ

вАЬThere isnвАЩt a deal thatвАЩs private, surely, about the mess oвАЩ things youвАЩve made on the marsh?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ The fight was really begun now, and Eliza turned in her seat, fixing her adversary with merciless eyes. Sarah could see very little but a monstrous blur, but she felt her malignant atmosphere in every nerve. She could hear the big, solid presence creaking with malice as it breathed, and had an impression of strained whalebone and stretching cloth. But it was always ElizaвАЩs most cherished garments that she visioned when they foughtвБ†вАФthe velvet gown that was folded away upstairsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ gloves, furs, and a feathered hat; furthest of all, the wedding-gown and the flaunting veil.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

вАЬPrivate!вАЭ Eliza repeated the sneered word as if it were something too precious to let go. вАЬThere canвАЩt be that much private about things as weвАЩve all on us known for years. What, folks has puzzled no end why youвАЩve never ended in tвАЩbankruptcy court long since! Will and me could likely haвАЩ tellt them about it, though, couldnвАЩt we, Sarah? Will anвАЩ me could easy haвАЩ tellt вАЩem why! Will and me could haвАЩ tellt where brass come from as was keeping you on tвАЩrailsвБ†вАФвАЭ

Will had been lending a careful ear to SimonвАЩs surly talk, but he lifted his head at the sound of his name.

вАЬNow, missis, just you let Mrs.¬†Simon be!вАЭ he admonished, with a troubled frown. вАЬYouвАЩre over fond of other folksвАЩ business by a deal.вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩll let her be and welcome, if sheвАЩll keep a civil tongue in her head!вАЭ Eliza cried. She went redder than ever, and slapped a teaspoon angrily on the cloth. вАЬBut if our brass isnвАЩt our business, IвАЩd like to know what is, and as for this stir about quitting Sandholes, itвАЩs nothing fresh, IвАЩm sure! We all on us know itвАЩs a marvel landlord didnвАЩt get shot on вАЩem long ago.вАЭ

The last remark galvanised Battersby into lively speech. Hitherto he had been busily concentrated on his food, but now his mean little features sharpened and his mean little eyes shone. He bent eagerly forward, leaning on the cloth, knife and fork erect like stakes in a snatched plot.

вАЬWhatвАЩs yon about quitting Sandholes?вАЭ he asked, in a thin voice. вАЬAre you thinking oвАЩ leaving, Simon? Is it true?вАЭ

вАЬI donвАЩt see as itвАЩs any affair oвАЩ yours if it is,вАЭ Simon answered him, with a sulky stare.

вАЬNay, it was nobbut a friendly question between man and man. If youвАЩre quitting the farm it would only be neighbourly just to give me a hint. ThereвАЩs a lad oвАЩ mine talking oвАЩ getting wed, and I thought as how SandholesвАЩd likely be going cheap. Has anybody put in for it yet wiвАЩ tвАЩagent, do ye think?вАЭ

вАЬNay, nor like to do, yet awhile,вАЭ Simon answered glumly, full of sullen hurt. All his love for his tiresome dwelling-place rose to the surface at this greed. вАЬI donвАЩt mind telling you, Mr.¬†Battersby, as you ax so kind, that I give in my notice but it wasnвАЩt took. Mr.¬†Dent would have it I mun think it over a bit more. Your ladвАЩll just have to bide or look out for somebody elseвАЩs shoes.вАЭ

This dreadful exhibition of meanness aggrieved Battersby almost to the verge of tears.

вАЬWell, now, if yon isnвАЩt dog-in-the-manger and nowt else!вАЭ he appealed to the company at large. вАЬWhat, youвАЩre late wiвАЩ your notice already, and yet youвАЩre for sitting tight to the farm like a hen on a pot egg! I shouldnвАЩt haвАЩ thought it of you, Simon, I shouldnвАЩt indeed. HereвАЩs a farmer wanting to quit and my lad wanting a farm, and yet the moment I ax a decent question I get sneck-posset geyly sharp. YouвАЩre jealous, thatвАЩs what it is, Simon; youвАЩre acting jealous-mean. YouвАЩve nobbut made a terble poor job oвАЩ things yourself, and you want to keep others from getting on anвАЩ all!вАЭ

Simon gave vent to an ironic laugh.

вАЬNay, now, Sam, never fret yourself!вАЭ he jeered. вАЬYou and your ladвАЩll get on right enough, IвАЩll be bound, what wiвАЩ your heaf-snatching and your sheep-grabbing and the rest oвАЩ your bonny ways! What, man, one oвАЩ your breedвАЩd be fair lost on a marsh farm, wiвАЩ nowt to lay hands on barrinвАЩ other folksвАЩ turmuts, and never a lile chance of an overlap!вАЭ

BattersbyвАЩs reputation was well known, and an irrepressible laugh greeted SimonвАЩs speech, but was instantly cut short by the terrible spectacle of the victimвАЩs face. Only the smug cousin went on laughing, because he was ignorant as well as smug, and did not know what a heaf meant, let alone how it was possible to add to it by SamвАЩs skilful if unlawful ways. Battersby jumped to his feet and thumped the table, so that the blue and gold china danced like dervishes from end to end. Mrs.¬†AddisonвАЩs tea made a waterfall down her second-best bodice, and SarahвАЩs heart, not being prepared for the thump, leaped violently into her mouth.

вАЬIвАЩll not be insulted in your spot nor nobody elseвАЩs,вАЭ he stormed at Will; вАЬnay, and IвАЩll not take telling from yon wastrel you call brother, neither! All on us know what a bonny mess oвАЩ things heвАЩs made at Sandholes. All on us know itвАЩll be right fain to see his back.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ As for you, you gomeless half-thick,вАЭ he added, swinging round so suddenly on the smug cousin that he was left gaping, вАЬyou can just shut yon calfвАЩs head oвАЩ yours and mighty sharp or IвАЩll shut it for you! Them as knows nowtвАЩd do best to say nowt, and look as lile like gawping jackasses as NatureвАЩll let вАЩem!вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ He sent a final glare round the stifled table, and let Eliza have the sting in his tail. вАЬIвАЩd been looking to be real friendly wiвАЩ Blindbeck,вАЭ he finished nastily, вАЬand my lad anвАЩ all, but I donвАЩt know as weвАЩll either on us be fain for it after this. Nay, I wainвАЩt set down agen, missis, and thatвАЩs flat, so you neednвАЩt ax me! IвАЩm off home and glad to be going, and no thanks to none oвАЩ you for nowt!вАЭ

He glanced at his plate to make certain there was nothing left, snatched at his cup and hastily swallowed the dregs; then, thrusting his chair backward so violently that it fell to the floor, he clapped his hat on his head and marched rudely out. Eliza, catching a glance from a tearful daughter, got to her feet, too. They swam from the room in a torrent of loud apologies and bitter, snarled replies.

Will leaned back in his chair with a fretted expression on his gentle face. The cousin, slowly turning from red to mottled mauve, observed to Mary Phyllis that the old manвАЩs language was вАЬreally remarkably like my chiefвАЩs!вАЭ Some of the younger end started to giggle afresh, but Sarah was still trembling from the unexpected shock, and Simon felt gloomy again after his public effort. He could see that he had upset Will, and that was the last thing he wanted to do, today. Will did not like Battersby, but he liked peace, and there were other reasons for friendly relations at present. WillвАЩs youngest daughter had a direct interest in BattersbyвАЩs lad and his hopes of a farm, and now the father had shaken the Blindbeck dust from his proud feet. She looked across at the cause of the trouble with tear-filled, indignant eyes.

вАЬSeems to me things is always wrong when you come to Blindbeck, Uncle Simon!вАЭ she exclaimed hotly. вАЬNobody wants your old farm, IвАЩm sure! I wouldnвАЩt have it at a gift! But you might have spoken him fair about it, all the same. I never see such folks as you and Aunt Sarah for setting other folk by the ears!вАЭ

Will said вАЬWhisht, lass, whisht!вАЭ in as cross a tone as he ever used to his girls, and Simon glowered at her sulkily, but he did not speak. She was a fair, pretty thing, with Geordie-anвАЩ-JimвАЩs eyes, and he did not wish to injure her happiness in any way. It was true enough, as she said, that there was generally something in the shape of a row as soon as he and Sarah set foot in the house, but he could not tell for the life of him how it came about. It could not be altogether their fault, he thought resentfully, yet with a sort of despair. Today, for instance, he had every reason for keeping the peace, and yet that fool of a Battersby must come jumping down his throat! Nobody could be expected to stand such manners and such nasty greedвБ†вАФgrabbing a manвАЩs homestead before his notice was well in! There was nothing surprising, of course, in the fact that the women had already come to blows. He had expected it from the start, and, with the resignation of custom, thought it as well over soon as late. They had had one scrap, as it was, from what Sarah had said, and the dregs of that pot of passion would still be hot enough to stir.

вАЬItвАЩs a shame, thatвАЩs what it is!вАЭ the girl was saying, over and over again. Tears dropped from the Geordie-anвАЩ-Jim eyes, and Simon felt furious with everybody, but particularly with himself.

вАЬYou neednвАЩt bother yourself,вАЭ he growled across at last, making a rough attempt to put the trouble right. вАЬYoung BattersbyвАЩs over much sense to go taking a spot like ourn, and as for his dad, heвАЩll be back afore you can speak. вАЩTisnвАЩt Sam Battersby, IвАЩll be bound, if he isnвАЩt as pleased as punch to be running in double harness wiвАЩ Blindbeck and its brass!вАЭ

вАЬAy, like other folk!вАЭ Eliza dropped on him from the clouds, reappearing panting from her chase. вАЬLike other folk a deal nearer home, Simon Thornthet, as you donвАЩt need telling! Battersby wanted nowt wiвАЩ the farmвБ†вАФhe tellt me so outside. вАЩTisnвАЩt good enough for the likes of him, nor for our Emily Marion, neither! He was that stamping mad he was for breaking it all off, but I got him promised to look in again next week. IвАЩd a deal oвАЩ work wiвАЩ him, all the same,вАЭ she added, flushing angrily at her brother-in-lawвАЩs ironic smile, вАЬand no thanks to you, neither, if I come out top, after all! Anyway, IвАЩll thank you to speak folk civilly at my table, if you can, whatever-like hired manвАЩs ways you keep for your own!вАЭ

She would have hectored him longer if Will had not got to his feet and taken himself and his brother out of the room, so instead she went back to her seat and drank a large cup of tea in angry gulps. Between drinks, however, she managed to say to the wife the things she had wanted to say to the man, though Sarah was silent and paid little or no heed. She wished she could have gone outside with the men, and helped to decide what her future was to be. But it was not for her to advise, who would soon be no better than a helpless log. It was her part to wait patiently until Simon fetched her away.

But it was not easy to wait at all in that atmosphere of critical dislike. The successive passages of arms had had their natural effect, and the party which had been so merry at the start was now in a state of boredom and constraint. The thoughts of most of those present were unfriendly towards the folk of the marsh, and Sarah could feel the thoughts winding about her in the air. Emily Marion was right, so they were saying in their minds; trouble always followed the Thornthwaites the moment they appeared. Storms arose out of nowhere and destroyed some festive occasion with a rush. Even to look at them, dowdy and disapproving, was to take the heart out of any happy day. It was certainly hard on the poor Will Thornthwaites that the tiresome Simons should dare to exist.

Sarah, bringing her mind back from the absent brothers with an effort, found the Method working again at top speed. The tea had soothed ElizaвАЩs nerves and stimulated her brain. She was now at her very best for behaving her very worst.

вАЬAnd so Mr.¬†AddisonвАЩs preaching next week, is he?вАЭ she reverted suddenly, making even that supreme egotist blink and start. Her Voice, furred and soft, reminded Sarah of a paw reaching out for someone to scratch. вАЬEh, now, but I should be in a rare twitter if it was Will as was setting up to preach! But there, weвАЩre none of us much of a hand at talking at our spot, and WillвАЩs summat better to do than just wagging a loose tongue. IвАЩll see the lads come along, though, as itвАЩs you, Mrs.¬†Addison, and an old friend, unless thereвАЩs summat useful theyвАЩre happen wanted for at home. Eh, Sarah, but wouldnвАЩt they talks to young men haвАЩ done a sight oвАЩ good to Geordie-anвАЩ-Jim? ItвАЩs a sad pity you didnвАЩt start preaching before they went, Mr.¬†AddisonвБ†вАФit is that! Like enough, if you had, theyвАЩd be at Sandholes yet.вАЭ

The preacherвАЩs brow had been thunderous during the early part of this speech, but now he looked suddenly coy. Sally, dropping her glance to her auntвАЩs lap, saw her fingers clench and unclench on a fold of her own black gown.

вАЬAny news of the prodigals?вАЭ Elliman Wilkinson suddenly enquired. He looked at Eliza as he spoke, and smiled as at a well-known joke. вАЬIвАЩm always in hopes to find one of them eating the fatted calf.вАЭ

вАЬNay, you must ask Sarah, not me!вАЭ Eliza answered, with an affected laugh. She despised Elliman in her heart, but she was grateful for the cue. вАЬSarah knows what theyвАЩre at, if thereвАЩs anybody does at all. Like enough theyвАЩll turn up one oвАЩ these days, but I donвАЩt know as weвАЩll run to calves. TheyвАЩll be terble rough in their ways, I doubt, after all this time. Out at elbows anвАЩ all, as like as not, and wiвАЩ happen a toe or two keeking through their boots!вАЭ

There was a ripple of laughter at this show of wit, and then Elliman, urged by a nudge and a whisper from Mary Phyllis, repeated the question in the proper quarter. He raised his voice when he spoke to Sarah, as if she were deaf as well as blind, and when she paused a moment before replying, he apostrophised her again. The whole table had pricked its ears and was listening by the time the answer came.

Sarah felt the giggles and the impertinent voice striking like arrows through the misty ring in which she sat. Sharpest of all was ElizaвАЩs laugh, introducing the question and afterwards punctuating it when it was put. She was achingly conscious of the antipathetic audience hanging on her lips. They were baiting her, and she knew it, and her heart swelled with helpless rage. A passionate longing seized her to be lord of them all for onceвБ†вАФjust for once to fling back an answer that would slay their smiles, put respect into their mocking voices and change their sneers into awed surprise. If only for once the Dream and the glory might be trueвБ†вАФthe trap and the new clothes and Geordie and the green front door! But nothing could be further from what they expected, as she knew too well. They were waiting merely to hear her say what she had often said beforeвБ†вАФfor news that there was no news or news that was worse than none. She had faced more than one trial that day, and had come out of them with her self-respect intact, but this unexpected humiliation was more than she could bear. She was telling herself in the pause that she would not answer at all, when something that she took for the total revolt of pride spoke to the mockers through her lips.

вАЬAy, but thereвАЩs rare good news!вАЭ she heard herself saying in a cheerful tone, and instantly felt her courage spring up and her heart lighten as the lie took shape. вАЬIвАЩd been saving it up, Eliza, for when we were by ourselves, but thereвАЩs no sense, I reckon, in not saying it straight out. GeordieвАЩs on his way home to England at this very minute, and he says heвАЩs a rare good lining to his jacket anвАЩ all!вАЭ

The air changed about her at once as she had always dreamed it would, and she heard the gasp of surprise pass from one to another like a quick-thrown ball. Eliza started so violently that she upset her cup and let it lie. She stared malevolently at the otherвАЩs face, her own set suddenly into heavy lines.

вАЬNay, but thatвАЩs news and no mistake!вАЭ she exclaimed, striving after her former tone, but without success. The note in her voice was clear to her blind hearer, sending triumphant shivers through her nerves.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬTell us again, will you, Sarah?вАЭ she added sharply. вАЬI doubt I heard you wrong.вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩll tell you and welcome till the cows come home!вАЭ Sarah said, with a sudden sprightliness that made the Wilkinson cousin open his eyes. It was almost as if another person had suddenly taken possession of SarahвАЩs place. There was a vitality about her that seemed to change her in every feature, an easy dignity that transformed the shabbiest detail of her dress. Her voice, especially, had changedвБ†вАФthat grudging, dully defiant voice. This was the warm, human voice of one who rejoiced in secret knowledge, and possessed her soul in perfect security and content.

вАЬHeвАЩs coming, I tell youвБ†вАФour GeordieвАЩs coming back!вАЭ The wonderful words seemed to fill her with strong courage every time she spoke. вАЬI canвАЩt rightly tell you when itвАЩll be, but he said we could look for him any minute now. Likely weвАЩll find him waiting at Sandholes when weвАЩve gitten home. HeвАЩs done well anвАЩ all, from what he says.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ IвАЩll be bound heвАЩs a rich man. He talks oвАЩ buying Sandholes, happenвБ†вАФor happen a bigger spot. I make no doubt heвАЩs as much brass asвАЩd buy Blindbeck out anвАЩ out!вАЭ

She fell silent again after this comprehensive statement, merely returning brief ayes and noes to the questions showered upon her from every side. Her air of smiling dignity, however, remained intact, and even her blind eyes, moving from one to another eager face, impressed her audience with a sense of truth. And then above the excited chatter there rose ElizaвАЩs voice, with the mother-note sounding faintly through the jealous greed.

вАЬYonвАЩs all very fine and large, Sarah, but what about my Jim? JimвАЩs made his pile anвАЩ all, I reckon, if GeordieвАЩs struck it rich. HeвАЩs as smart as Geordie, is our Jim, any day oвАЩ the week! Hark ye, Sarah! What about my Jim?вАЭ

Quite suddenly Sarah began to tremble, exactly as if the other had struck her a sharp blow. She shrank instantly in her chair, losing at once her dignity and ease. The fine wine of vitality ran out of her as out of a crushed grape, leaving only an empty skin for any malignant foot to stamp into the earth. She tried to speak, but could find no voice brave enough to meet the fierce rain of ElizaвАЩs words. A mist other than that of blindness came over her eyes, and with a lost movement she put out a groping, shaking hand. Sally, in a sudden access of pity, gathered it in her own.

She slid her arm round her aunt, and drew her, tottering and trembling, to her feet.

вАЬItвАЩs overmuch for her, thatвАЩs what it is,вАЭ she said kindly, but taking care to avoid her motherвАЩs angry glance. вАЬItвАЩs knocked her over, coming that sudden, and no wonder, either. Come along, Aunt Sarah, and sit down for a few minutes in the parlour. YouвАЩll be as right as a bobbin after youвАЩve had a rest.вАЭ

She led her to the door, a lithe, upright figure supporting trembling age, and EllimanвАЩs eyes followed her, so that for once he was heedless of Mary Phyllis when she spoke. Most of the company, indeed, had fallen into a waiting silence, as if they knew that the act was not yet finished, and that the cue for the curtain still remained to be said. And the instinct that held them breathless was perfectly sound, for in the square of the door Sarah halted herself and turned. Her worn hands gripped her gown on either side, and if May had been there to see her, she would again have had her impression of shrouded flame. She paused for a moment just to be sure of her breath, and then her voice went straight with her blind glance to the point where Eliza sat.

вАЬJimвАЩs dead, I reckon!вАЭ she said, clearly and cruellyвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬay, I doubt heвАЩs dead. GeordieвАЩd never be coming without him if he was over sod. YouвАЩd best make up your mind, Eliza, as heвАЩs dead and gone!вАЭ

It was the voice of an oracle marking an open grave, of Cassandra, crying her knowledge in Troy streets. It held them all spellbound until she had gone out. Even Eliza was silent for once on her red plush chair.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶
